title: Relativistic transformation of temperature revisited
abstract: The relativistic transformation of temperature has long remained controversial, with the classical laws of Planck-Einstein, Ott-Eddington-Moller and Landsberg yielding conflicting results. We reexamine this issue from a relativistic thermodynamic and statistical perspective, starting from the energy-momentum tensor of an isotropic system and defining the effective temperature Teff as that inferred by a moving observer from the transformed energy density. Analyses of a photon gas, a relativistic ideal gas and an electron gas show that Teff consistently increases with velocity, supporting the Ott-Eddington interpretation while depending on the system's equation of state. These results indicate that temperature is not a Lorentz-invariant scalar but an observer-dependent quantity. A consistent relativistic description emerges when temperature is related to the inverse-temperature four-vector beta, linking operational and invariant viewpoints within a unified thermodynamic framework.
summary: This paper re-examines the relativistic transformation of temperature using a rigorous thermodynamic approach based on the energy-momentum tensor. It demonstrates that temperature is not a Lorentz invariant scalar, showing that the effective temperature measured by a moving observer increases with velocity. The work unifies these concepts by introducing the inverse-temperature four-vector into a consistent relativistic framework.
main_problem: The paper addresses the long-standing controversy regarding the relativistic transformation of temperature, as classical laws yield conflicting results.
main_result: The effective temperature inferred by a moving observer consistently increases with velocity, supporting the Ott-Eddington interpretation, and temperature is shown to be an observer-dependent quantity.
method: The analysis uses a relativistic thermodynamic and statistical framework, starting from the energy-momentum tensor of an isotropic perfect fluid and defining an effective temperature from transformed energy densities.
model_or_system: The study analyzes three paradigmatic systems: a photon gas (blackbody radiation), a relativistic ideal gas (Maxwell-Jüttner), and a relativistic electron gas (Fermi-Dirac statistics).
key_observables: Effective temperature ($T_{	ext{eff}}$), the ratio $T_{	ext{eff}}/T$, and the inverse-temperature four-vector ($ eta_\mu$).
important_parameters: Observer velocity ($v$), system equation of state ($P/ho$), and the mass-to-temperature ratio ($m/T$).
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes the standard energy-momentum tensor for a perfect fluid, neglecting dissipative effects like heat conduction or shear viscosity.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the smooth interpolation of the effective temperature ratio ($T_{	ext{eff}}/T$) versus velocity for various particle mass regimes, confirming the dependence on the equation of state.
paper_structure: The paper reviews conflicting classical transformation laws, develops the general formalism using the energy-momentum tensor, analyzes specific gases (photon, ideal, Fermi), and concludes by unifying the description via the inverse-temperature four-vector.
why_interesting: While focused on classical thermodynamics, the rigorous handling of observer-dependence and the covariant formulation of temperature are foundational concepts relevant to relativistic quantum field theory and non-equilibrium statistical mechanics.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
